chunks.title: Personal Experience with Distance Running
chunks.summary: The speaker shares their background as a distance runner and a vegan, describing the physical toll it took on their body, including a significant loss of muscle mass. They reflect on the endorphin rush associated with running and the potential health issues faced by long-distance runners.
chunks.transcript: i've spoken about this on my social media and i can always get a blow but a lot of blow back when i try and throw shade at like distance running but i used to be distance runner too yeah so there was a time in my life where i was a vegan and i was running distance you can speak to both of them then yeah and i was 20 to 25 pounds of muscle mass lighter so you were pretty thin i'm a moderately muscular human right now but i was an extremely under muscled human at that point but i enjoy i'm sort of addicted to the endorphin rush of running i suspect you may understand this yeah yeah and when i at that point i was a physician assistant working in cardiology and i saw multiple distance runners come in with atrial fibrillation arrhythmias one marathon isn't going to do it to most any humans right right but like distance runners i was a distance from a long time it's a  addiction you know this is this right and and i saw a lot of marathoners come in with arvd which is a rhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia so you get you get infiltration of fatty tissue into the right ventricle of the heart with like extremely long distance running and why are we not surprised yes humans may have done some persistence hunting but persistence hunting is like walking right carefully quietly yeah delicately yeah with the bow and arrow or something yeah when i was with the hazard like we walk and then we sprint and then we walk in the sprint you don't run 26 miles at seven to six to eight minutes a mile that's not good for humans and we know that that creates all sorts of problems

chunks.title: Health Issues Related to Distance Running
chunks.summary: The discussion highlights health complications observed in distance runners, such as atrial fibrillation and other arrhythmias. The speaker mentions their personal experiences with ultra-running and the adverse effects, indicating that extensive running can lead to severe health problems.

chunks.title: Cortisol and Stress in Athletes
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the role of cortisol and stress in athletic performance, emphasizing how chronic high stress can lead to hormone disruptions. The speaker recalls their own cortisol pattern and discusses the need to address underlying stressors beyond just adrenal health.
chunks.transcript: you were last year probably everything so my cortisol would spike in the middle of the night because you test you basically you do it when you um like before dinner before bed in the middle of the night if you get up in the middle of the night first thing when you wake up and a couple hours after that so kind of test this like tracks your pattern and my middle of the night draw sample was the highest and then it went down from there it never went up when i woke up it just kept going down so now my new pattern is the way it's supposed to be but it's basically the bottom of the level i guess the bottom lowest level but it's in the right shape now what would cause like a hormone disruption like what would cause a cortisol um pattern disruption this is a check engine light in your car this is like something is going on in the car stress accumulated stress your stress bucket is overflowing so people people chase this and they say i have adrenal dysfunction and it's like well you have too much stress and you're not recovering which can be a million things right it can be inadequate nutrients it can be poor sleep it can be trauma it can be you know all kinds of things heavy metal toxicity mold toxicity breast implants that are causing your immune system it can be exposure to other petrochemicals from racing it can be exposed all kinds of things so it's like this indicator um i don't think that the adrenal stuff is the first thing to like go address with someone it's like okay something is messed up that person needs basically like a vacation and a lot of people can't take that so you have to kind of figure out like what

chunks.title: Hormetic Stress and Its Implications
chunks.summary: The segment introduces the concept of hormesis, defining it as the beneficial effects of low doses of stressors on the body. The speaker questions the extent of beneficial stress and its relation to nutrition and exercise, particularly concerning the consumption of plant-based foods.

chunks.title: Plant Compounds and Thyroid Function
chunks.summary: Exploration of specific plant compounds, particularly isothiocyanates found in cruciferous vegetables, and their effects on human health. The speaker highlights potential negative impacts on thyroid function due to these compounds, suggesting that not all vegetables are beneficial for everyone.

chunks.title: Complexity of Human Physiology
chunks.summary: The speaker wraps up by discussing the complexities of human physiology and the diverse effects of various foods on health. They stress that while some individuals may thrive on plant-based diets, others could experience adverse reactions, advocating for a more tailored approach to nutrition.
chunks.transcript: well it's like just understand that like not all vegetables are good for all people and that's what i try to help people understand so your question is really well taken like hormesis maybe it's a thing in humans it seems to be right because you go to the gym you exercise that's a stressor and then you get stronger right you go in the sun you get stronger in some ways you get these there we know there are some stressors sure yeah there are some things that are a little bit of stress is good for us but i don't think we understand how big our hormis stress bucket is for a lot of people like you go in the sauna you've got a sauna over there in your rehab bedroom you go in the sauna and the sun is a hormetic stressor too we know that cortisol goes up after you go in the sauna and so it's like well how much can any one body take of all these things and what we don't really have a good foundational metric to be like you're
